prompt: When launching a Third-Party Risk Management program, what is the most important first step in defining vendor risk scope?
choices: Import all vendors and assign the same risk tier
Identify which vendor relationships, services, and data flows are in scope for formal assessment
Assess only vendors with expiring contracts
Defer scope decisions until after questionnaires are sent
explanation: TPRM starts with clear scope boundaries so assessments target the right third parties, services, and data exposure paths.

prompt: Which vendor population is typically prioritized when defining initial TPRM scope?
choices: Vendors with no system access or data handling
Vendors with critical services, privileged access, or sensitive data processing
Only new vendors created this month
Only vendors with active incidents
explanation: Criticality, access level, and data sensitivity are common criteria for prioritizing scoped third parties.

prompt: In TPRM, why should business owners be involved in vendor risk scoping?
choices: They can approve all findings without review
They determine legal language for every contract
They understand service dependency and operational impact if a vendor fails
They replace the risk team in platform administration
explanation: Business owners provide context on process dependencies and outage impact, which improves scope accuracy.

prompt: What scoping outcome best supports repeatable vendor risk assessments?
choices: A documented classification model linking vendor types to assessment requirements
A one-time spreadsheet of vendor names
Manual assessment decisions made per analyst preference
No segmentation until annual audits
explanation: Documented scope categories enable consistent trigger criteria and repeatable assessment workflows.

prompt: A TPRM team wants to avoid over-assessing low-impact vendors. Which scope control helps most?
choices: Require full due diligence for every vendor regardless of profile
Skip all assessments for internal suppliers
Use inherent risk screening to determine whether full assessment is required
Assess only vendors selected by procurement
explanation: Inherent risk screening helps right-size assessment effort based on business and security impact indicators.

prompt: In Vulnerability Response, what is the primary objective of vulnerability intake?
choices: Collect and normalize scanner findings into actionable vulnerability records
Close vulnerabilities automatically after import
Create incidents for every scan result
Ignore duplicate findings across sources
explanation: Intake focuses on bringing external findings into VR with consistent normalization and deduplication for triage.

prompt: Why is source integration quality important during vulnerability intake?
choices: It ensures scan data includes reliable asset and finding context used in downstream prioritization
It removes the need for remediation tasks
It allows analysts to bypass state transitions
It guarantees zero false positives
explanation: Good source data quality improves correlation, ownership mapping, and triage confidence.

prompt: What intake behavior reduces duplicate remediation effort in VR?
choices: Creating separate vulnerability items for identical findings on the same asset each import
Disabling normalization rules
Using correlation and deduplication logic across repeated scanner observations
Deleting previous findings before each scan cycle
explanation: Correlation and deduplication prevent duplicate work and keep vulnerability state history coherent.

prompt: Which data attribute is most critical to preserve at intake for effective assignment?
choices: Scanner UI color theme
Asset ownership and CI relationship details
Analyst personal priority notes
Dashboard widget layout preference
explanation: Assignment and accountability depend on accurate asset-to-owner and CI mappings captured during intake.

prompt: A vulnerability feed starts creating unusable records with missing fields. What is the best first response?
choices: Close all imported vulnerabilities as false positives
Pause or adjust the integration mapping and validation rules before continuing imports
Manually edit every record after intake
Disable all scanner connectors permanently
explanation: Fixing integration mappings and validation controls at intake prevents recurring downstream triage defects.

prompt: What is the main purpose of Customer Service Management in ServiceNow?
choices: Manage employee payroll and benefits
Handle customer interactions, cases, and issue resolution across channels
Replace CMDB discovery tools
Schedule on-site field technicians only
explanation: CSM centralizes customer service operations, case workflows, and communication channels for faster resolution.

prompt: When first exploring CSM, which record type is the core unit of customer issue tracking?
choices: Change request
Incident
Case
Knowledge feedback
explanation: The case is the central transaction in CSM for managing customer-reported issues and service requests.

prompt: Why do organizations adopt account and contact models in CSM?
choices: To track software license metering
To define customer relationships and route cases to the correct support context
To replace HR profiles
To remove need for service level definitions
explanation: Account and contact data provide customer context used for entitlement checks, routing, and communications.

prompt: Which CSM capability helps deflect repetitive customer questions before a case is opened?
choices: Vulnerability calculators
Stockroom transfers
Knowledge and self-service portal experiences
Contract line depreciation schedules
explanation: Knowledge and self-service enable customers to resolve common issues without agent intervention.

prompt: What is a recommended first metric when evaluating early CSM implementation success?
choices: Mean time to resolution for customer cases
Number of CMDB classes created
Total hardware assets retired
Count of background scripts run daily
explanation: Case resolution time is a direct indicator of service efficiency and customer-facing operational performance.

prompt: In Field Service Management, what is the role of a work order?
choices: A financial ledger entry for invoicing only
A parent record used to coordinate field tasks, scheduling, and fulfillment
A replacement for customer contracts
A dashboard widget for dispatchers
explanation: Work orders organize service work and act as the control record for downstream field activities.

prompt: Why are work order tasks used in FSM implementations?
choices: To split a service job into executable units that can be assigned and tracked independently
To disable dispatching for multi-step jobs
To avoid capturing completion details
To replace all case records in CSM
explanation: Tasks break down field jobs into manageable actions with clear ownership and status progression.

prompt: Which information on a work order most directly improves dispatch outcomes?
choices: Analyst theme preference
Unrelated contract attachments only
A blank short description
Detailed location, required skills, and service time window
explanation: Dispatcher decisions depend on location, skill requirements, and timing constraints captured on work records.

prompt: A team wants better lifecycle visibility for field jobs. What should they standardize first?
choices: Personal notes format for each technician
Consistent state model for work orders and work order tasks
Manual emailing instead of status updates
Closing all tasks at creation time
explanation: Standard state transitions provide reliable reporting and operational control for work execution.

prompt: What is the main reason to relate a work order to a customer account or location record?
choices: To avoid SLA calculations
To provide service context and history across repeat visits
To prevent mobile updates
To disable inventory checks
explanation: Account and location relationships preserve context for planning, communication, and repeat service quality.

prompt: What is the primary goal of HR Service Delivery in ServiceNow?
choices: Manage customer incident response
Support HR processes through standardized services, cases, and employee experiences
Replace payroll engines
Track software vulnerabilities
explanation: HRSD digitizes and standardizes HR services, improving employee support and process consistency.

prompt: In HRSD, which record typically tracks fulfillment of an employee HR request?
choices: Problem record
HR case
Work order
Vendor profile
explanation: HR cases are the primary fulfillment records for employee issues and HR service requests.

prompt: Why are HR services important in an HRSD implementation?
choices: They define structured request types and fulfillment patterns for HR teams
They replace legal counsel
They remove security role requirements
They bypass knowledge management
explanation: HR services standardize intake and delivery so recurring HR requests are handled consistently.

prompt: What is a key reason HRSD uses employee center experiences?
choices: To force all requests through email
To provide a unified self-service interface for HR help, knowledge, and request submission
To hide HR policies from employees
To store vulnerability scan outputs
explanation: Employee center channels HR demand through a consistent portal and improves self-service adoption.

prompt: When introducing HRSD, what governance practice should be established early?
choices: Allow unrestricted access to all HR case data
Disable assignment groups for HR
Define case confidentiality and role-based access controls for sensitive employee records
Store all case notes in public knowledge
explanation: HR data is sensitive, so role-based access and confidentiality controls are foundational to compliant operations.
